title_en: Marriage and Commerce in Tuhfat al-Muhtaj
content_en:
The marriage and commercial law chapters of Tuhfat al-Muhtaj represent Ibn Hajar al-Haytami's most extended contribution to Shafi'i private law, addressing the practical questions of Muslim communities across a vast geographic range. His fatwas on marriage and commercial questions were sought from Egypt to Southeast Asia, and Tuhfat al-Muhtaj incorporates the analytical framework that generated those responses.

Ibn Hajar al-Haytami's treatment of the invalid marriage contract and its consequences is particularly thorough. A marriage lacking a required condition (such as the wali's presence or the two witnesses) is invalid (fasid or batil in Shafi'i terminology). He addresses what happens to the couple who have lived as husband and wife in an invalid marriage: the marriage must be dissolved, any sexual relations that occurred while the invalidity was unknown are not treated as fornication (shubuha — error — prevents the application of had punishment), and the wife is entitled to mahr al-mithl as compensation for the intimacy that occurred.

The chapter on divorce presents the Shafi'i school's position on the triple talaq with its evidential basis. Three pronouncements of divorce — whether in one sitting or across three separate instances — produce the major irrevocable divorce (ba'in kubra). The Shafi'i school's position that three pronouncements in one sitting count as three is defended with the historical evidence that the Companions treated it as such until 'Umar ibn al-Khattab's decree, and that 'Umar's decree was a policy decision (siyasah) to deter hasty divorce rather than a reversal of the legal ruling.

Ibn Hajar al-Haytami's treatment of the mut'ah (gift of consolation) after divorce without specified mahr or before consummation reflects the Shafi'i position that it is obligatory (wajib) in some circumstances and recommended in others. The Quranic verse (2:236) establishing mut'ah as obligatory when divorce occurs before consummation and without a specified mahr is cited as the primary evidence.

In commercial law, Tuhfat al-Muhtaj addresses with particular depth the question of trading in items whose ultimate use may be prohibited. Ibn Hajar al-Haytami's strict position — that a seller who knows their product will be used for prohibited purposes is legally complicit in the prohibition — had practical consequences for commerce in his era and has continued relevance today. He extends this to the case of selling neutral items to someone who is known to be committed to their prohibited use, applying the principle that intent and knowledge affect legal character.

Ibn Hajar al-Haytami's chapter on gharar (excessive uncertainty) in commercial contracts provides the conceptual framework for evaluating novel contract types. He identifies the key criterion for impermissible gharar: the uncertainty must be: present in the subject matter itself (not merely about market conditions), significant enough to generate dispute and harm, and avoidable without defeating the contract's commercial purpose. This tripartite criterion provides a useful analytical tool for evaluating whether a novel commercial arrangement falls within the Islamic prohibition of gharar.
